sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 49, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at r32-36, c14-18. Red path visible at r38-42, c14-18 (left vertical) and r40, c14-38 (horizontal bottom).
- Goal model: Navigate to goal at r49-55, c43-49.
- Action model: Player moves within black corridors. Red path may be the path to follow.
- Plan: Go DOWN to reach the red path at r38-42.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 48 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
DOWN didn't work - player stayed at r32-36, c14-18. The destination at r38-42 is the red path, which is not a black corridor. So the player can't move DOWN to the red path.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. The player can only move within black corridors. The red path is not a black corridor, so the player can't move onto it directly.
Maybe the player needs to use SPACE to teleport to the red path or to create a gray platform that connects to the red path.
Let me try SPACE to see if it teleports the player to a different position.
Actually, looking at the maze more carefully:
r38: WBRRRRRBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWBRBWWWWWW
r39: WBBRRRRBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRBWWWWWW
r40: WBRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRBWWWWWW
r41: WBBRRRRBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWW
r42: WBRRRRRBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW
The red path forms a U-shape. The left vertical segment is at r38-42, c14-18. The horizontal bottom is at r40, c14-38. The right vertical segment is at r9-40, c39-41.
The player is at r32-36, c14-18, which is just above the left vertical segment of the red path.
Looking at r37: WBBBBBBBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWBRBWWWWWW
At r37, the black corridor at c14-18 ends. Below it is the red path at r38-42.
So the player can't move DOWN because the destination is the red path, not a black corridor.
Maybe the player needs to move RIGHT from the current position to reach the right vertical segment of the red path.
Looking at r32-36, the player is at c14-18. To the right is a white area (WWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW) at c20-38, then B at c39, R at c40, B at c41.
The player can't move RIGHT because the destination is white.
Hmm, the player seems stuck at r32-36, c14-18. The player can't move DOWN (red path), RIGHT (white), or LEFT (white/black wall).
Let me try SPACE to see if it teleports the player to a different position.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at r32-36, c14-18. DOWN blocked by red path. RIGHT blocked by white area. Player stuck.
Goal model: Navigate to goal at r49-55, c43-49.
Action model: Player moves within black corridors. SPACE teleports player.
Plan: Try SPACE to teleport to a different position, possibly near the red path or goal.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try SPACE to teleport
result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"After SPACE: done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for node in nodes:
    if node['color'] == 'b' and node['pixels'] == 24:
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Player: r{min(rows)}-{max(rows)} c{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check surroundings
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
pr = [n for n in nodes if n['color']=='b' and n['pixels']==24][0]
pr_min, pr_max = min([p[0] for p in pr['boundary']]), max([p[0] for p in pr['boundary']])
pc_min, pc_max = min([p[1] for p in pr['boundary']]), max([p[1] for p in pr['boundary']])
print(f"\nAround player:")
for r in range(max(0,pr_min-2), min(64,pr_max+10)):
    print(f"r{r:2d}: {ascii_lines[r][pc_min-2:pc_max+30]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
